sentences.text: I did, quite extensively, and have had contact with the family of the-- Sugihara's sons and wife.
And of course, in general, from a point of view of recognizing-- excuse me-- that here was a great man.
I don't think, directly, I did, because I, I was, as a child, of course, in a environment that was very much involved with Jewish life in Chicago.
I grew up, of course, in Chicago once we settled here.
And my parents kept me very much involved with Jewish life.
And Jewish life had a lot to do with the Holocaust.

You couldn't live in Jewish society from the 1940s on, through the present day, without having constant contact with the events that had happened during the Holocaust in Europe, and so I was immersed in this.
In fact, I recited poetry at events commemorizing-- commemorating the uprising or the events in the ghetto, and so forth and so on.
But the only connection, in terms of Sugihara, was that he was the doorway to escape.
I didn't otherwise contemplate Sugihara, in response to your question, only that this was the avenue in which we, fortunately, were able to escape the, the consequences of the Holocaust.
And only by virtue of my father's making all the right decisions along the way and Sugihara giving him this singular opportunity did I connect up the events.
So more important to me was the events in Poland, then in Russia and in Europe, as it relates to the Holocaust, than just that moment of opportunity that gave us the avenue to escape.
It was in more adult years than in my growing-up time.
In-- I became involved in financial-- in the financial world.
And as a consequence of that, I gave lectures in Japan.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]

When, when I began to give lectures on financial matters in Japan, and when, as chairman of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, we opened a permanent office in Tokyo, and I travel to Japan quite often, I, of course, became very conscious of the fact that Japan had an affinity to me that was different than everyone else.
Here I was, alive, and a leader of finance in the United States, by virtue of the fact that once, some years ago, a Japanese gentleman gave me this opportunity, as a child, to survive.
And I began, in my speeches, to talk about this event to many crowds in the financial arena of Japan.

When I would say to them, you know, I am a survivor of the Holocaust simply because a gentleman named Sugihara saved my life-- and this was of great interest in a introduction of a speech, you can imagine, on financial matters, when I'm talking about one of their own.
And I used that.
As a consequence of that, people began to write that story and write what I was saying in the news.
And I would get interviewed, and so my connection began to grow stronger and stronger, as it related to Sugihara.
Oh, I know, definitely, it was in the middle '70s that it began, because that was then that we begin to, as the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, become international.
And we moved into connection to Japan.

And so my first actual foray as a speaker was with the Nobel laureate in economics, my good friend Milton Friedman, who invited me to, to share a stage with him, which was a great honor in itself, and speak about future markets.
And that was the beginning, and it began in the middle to late '70s.
Well, they contacted me.
I, I didn't actually go about trying because it was an event that occurred.
The son, Hiroki, called me, and we met in Tokyo, and we discussed, you know, mutual events about this whole episode.
And I've kept track with Hiroki since that time, and with that family since that time.
No, I never did.
He passed away before I had a chance.
Well, no, because the contact that occurred with Hiroki must have been in the early '80s.
And by then, the, the father was way up in the years, and there was no contact.
Oh, yes.
Definitely.
I don't know.
I don't know the politics involved.
I do know that the, the press was very interested.
The media did follow the story and always encouraged me with questions about my memory of Sugihara, because to them it was a moment of great pride.
Here was a, a Japanese person that saved my life, along with many others.
Oh, absolutely-- I, I, of course, knew the name quite well.
And, as I say, we did have contact with the family, but nothing with Sugihara himself, and certainly no government official has ever contacted me as it relates to this story.
Well, I think there is an easy answer, because it had to be a humanitarian call inside, some justice demanding that he do the right thing.
There was no other reason for him to do it.
In fact, there was every reason for him not to do it.
We know the story.
We know that the Foreign Ministry of Japan told him not to issue visas.
And we know the history of that period of time with Japan, and the Germans being allies, that that was not the thing to do, to issue visas, particularly to Jews who were escaping their fate.

And so the, the only motivation that Sugihara could have possibly had was one of humanitarian spirit, devotion to justice, and, I guess, a, a sympathy to the plight of people who were caught and would be suffering simply because they were Jews.
Well, I don't think it's unusual for the Japanese.
I, I don't believe that the Japanese, as a people, are, are different than that.
I, I have found-- of course, now I, I know many Japanese.
I'm friends with many Japanese and have discussed this with many.
And I have found that this quality of a sympathy, of a, of a basic understanding of human rights, as it were, is not uncommon in Japan.
Sugihara found himself in a position where he could do something, as he said, to carry out the dictates of his god rather than the dictates of his country, officially, of his government, and did.

But most Japanese that I've run into, at least from what I've been able to see, understood and understand today the plight of the Jews, and the sufferings that were committed to them only because they were Jews.
Well, actually, it has because, as you probably know, I am a director of the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington DC.

I did that with the express reason that I felt it was my obligation to make sure that this story-- not just the Sugihara story, of course-- but that the memory of the Holocaust was maintained, so that it would prevent it from occurring again.
Only through memory can we prevent making-- the world making the same mistake again and again.
And obviously, I wanted, also, to preserve the name of Sugihara in the process of memorializing the events of the Holocaust, to prevent their reoccurrence.
And I believe that it is through education of this sort, both in visual form at the museum, as well as in written and academic form otherwise, that we can prevent such a reoccurrence.
Unfortunately, it continues to plague humankind.
There are always those who would commit genocide.
Rwanda comes to mind.
And although it isn't on the same magnitude as was the Holocaust the Jews suffered, but nevertheless, it has the genocidal incidents that make it similar.
And unfortunately, these things and events will, again, happen if we are not always on guard against the seeds of hatred being allowed to grow anywhere in the world, to any people of the world.
I wish I could tell you that there will be more incidences of that than there have been.
I don't know.
We know that during the-- during the war-- during the Holocaust, there were precious few that stepped out.
Raoul Wallenberg, of course, is well-remembered.
Schindler is well-remembered, and Sugihara.
But there is a handful of those against the millions that did nothing and, in fact, had a deaf ear and a blind eye to all that occurred.
In fact, that is what bothered me, as an adult, more than anything.
Where were the religious leaders of the world?
Where were the philosophers, academicians?
Where were the government officials?
Where were the average people who should have protested and not allowed?
And, and their voice alone should have torn down the walls of the ghetto.
Nobody was there.
And so one has to be a little skeptical about the-- this ability of changing human nature and making people speak out in anger when they see an atrocity of this sort.
My hope is, though, that we can change.
And education is the only way that, perhaps, will cause that change.
We have to, as Jews, be more mindful, and we are, of these events and be the teachers.
Maybe we were the chosen people for many reasons.
But certainly, the reason of teaching, and of memory, and of bringing out the best in the world is our mission, at least I feel that way.
Well, I think the story of Sugihara is one direct method of showing.
Here's a-- what he did, how he stood up, how he was dictated by his conscience, and what was the result?
I mean, look at the result of the lives saved.
And you know the saying about saving one life saves the entire generation.
He has done that.
And I think we can show, in vivid form, what, what the value of such a courage and such a humanitarianism means.

I think we have to show that in conjunction with what happened to those that weren't fortunate enough to fall in, in a way-- I mean to find themselves in, in a way that they could be saved by a Sugihara, because most of the millions weren't that lucky, as I was.

And so we have to show their story, and we have to use that as an antithesis-- the, the black and white, as it were, of when a human being intervenes to save the lives, versus when the rest of the world does not intervene and allows the atrocity.
I think that's the only message you can give, and you have to educate the young people, I think, is where this begins.
It doesn't begin with adults.
It begins at a much younger age.
Kids in their teens should know the difference and be told the difference.
Well, I guess risk has a counterpart.
It's called reward.
One doesn't take risks unless there is a reward.
Sugihara's reward was his self-esteem, his conscience, his, his religion, his belief in right from wrong.
Those are big rewards for a human being, because they serve a need-- an internal need, that give you great strength, without which I think you can't get anywhere.
And so I think that there is a definite risk-reward of that kind for every individual that understands the difference between hatred and a savior or saving lives.
I don't think it's so difficult a message.
And I don't think that it's so difficult to understand that the reward is much greater than the risks involved and well-worth the risk involved.
Sugihara is not alone, as I say.
There are others, and there's probably countless of different kinds of others that helped save lives during the Holocaust, whose names we'll never know, whether they saved one, or two, or a handful.
We just know the standouts.
We know the, the people who saved in the thousands of people.
But there were others, and I think we have to show those stories as well, to show that the risks that they took were rewarded in their lives, and I think it were-- yeah, I think it was.
Well, I can only contemplate and hope that the answer is yes.
One doesn't know what one's moral strengths are in, in a moment of difficulty until you're there.
I'd like to think that I would be up to that challenge.
I think I would, but, you know, until I'm there and would have to make that decision, I-- I can't tell you with 100% certainty.
I think I would.
No, I think you've got it.
We covered it.
